steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage gives two rules: (1) listeners play their favorite recordings at higher volumes than less-liked ones, and (2) musicians tend to listen at higher volumes than nonmusicians. The scenario: both a musician and a nonmusician share the same favorite ("Sad but True"), and the musician plays it at 85.5 dB. Rule 2 supports the inference that the nonmusician would likely play it at a lower volume.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the musician and nonmusician would both play other music by Metallica at approximately 85.5 dB: The passage says favorite recordings get the highest volume; other Metallica songs are not stated to be favorites, so their volume cannot be predicted this way. ✗
  • Choice B — the musician would not play music in the heavy metal genre at less than 85.5 dB: Only the favorite is played at the peak volume; other heavy metal songs the musician likes less would logically be played at lower volumes. ✗
  • Choice C — the nonmusician would play "Sad but True" at greater than 85.5 dB: This reverses the pattern — nonmusicians tend to listen at lower volumes than musicians, not higher. ✗
  • Choice D — the nonmusician would play "Sad but True" at less than 85.5 dB: Since musicians tend to listen louder than nonmusicians, the nonmusician's volume for the same song would likely be below the musician's 85.5 dB. ✓
That matches Choice D — the nonmusician would play "Sad but True" at less than 85.5 dB.
